content:
# Revelation Chapter 15 -- The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, pages 288-289 | **Source Image:** REV_015_page_001.jpg, REV_015_page_002.jpg

1. And I saw another sign in heaven, great and marvelous: seven angels having seven plagues, the last ones, because in them the wrath of God is completed.

2. And I saw something like a sea of glass mingled with fire, and those who had overcome the beast and its image and the number of its name, standing upon the sea of glass, having harps of God.

3. And they sing the song of Moses, the servant of God, and the song of the Lamb, saying, "Great and marvelous are your works, Lord God Almighty; righteous and true are your ways, O King of the nations.

4. Who shall not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name? For you alone are holy; for all the nations shall come and worship before you, for your righteous judgments have been made manifest."

5. And after these things I looked, and the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened,

6. and the seven angels who had the seven plagues came out of the temple, clothed in pure bright linen, and girded around their chests with golden sashes.

7. And one of the four living creatures gave to the seven angels seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God who lives forever and ever.

8. And the temple was filled with smoke from the glory of God and from his power, and no one was able to enter the temple until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed.

---
## SOPHIA's Council Notes

**15:4** -- All three translators conveyed the same theological content for this verse: God alone is holy, all nations will worship, and his righteous acts have been revealed. The core phrasing was remarkably consistent across all three, with differences limited to word choice (see Disagreement Log).

**15:8** -- All three translators agreed closely on this verse. The temple filled with smoke from God's glory, no one able to enter until the plagues were completed. Differences were purely stylistic.

---
## Disagreement Log

**15:1 -- "was completed" vs. "is completed" vs. "reaches its completion":**
- EMET: "in them was completed the wrath of the God"
- LOGOS: "in them the wrath of God is completed"
- PNEUMA: "with these plagues God's wrath reaches its completion"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "the wrath of God is completed." The Greek aorist passive etelesthē can be rendered as either past or gnomic present in English; the present tense (LOGOS) better captures the prophetic certainty. PNEUMA's expansion "reaches its completion" adds words.

**15:1 -- "the last ones" vs. "the final plagues":**
- EMET: "seven plagues, the last ones"
- LOGOS: "seven plagues, the last ones"
- PNEUMA: "the seven final plagues"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with the appositional "the last ones," preserving the Greek structure where tas eschatas stands in apposition.

**15:2 -- "conquering out of" vs. "had overcome" vs. "had triumphed over":**
- EMET: "the ones conquering out of the beast and out of the image of it and out of the number of the name of it"
- LOGOS: "those who had overcome the beast and his image and the number of his name"
- PNEUMA: "those who had triumphed over the beast, over its image, and over the number of its name"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "those who had overcome." EMET's "conquering out of" is too literal for the triple ek construction. PNEUMA's "triumphed over" is viable but "overcome" is the more standard biblical term for nikao.

**15:2 -- "as a sea of glass having been mixed with fire" vs. "something like a sea of glass mingled with fire":**
- EMET: "as a sea of glass having been mixed with fire"
- LOGOS: "something like a sea of glass mingled with fire"
- PNEUMA: "what looked like a sea of glass shot through with fire"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. "Something like" renders the Greek hos naturally. PNEUMA's "shot through with" is vivid but interpretive.

**15:2 -- Pronoun for the beast:**
- EMET: "of it" throughout
- LOGOS: "his" throughout
- PNEUMA: "its" throughout
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "its" (PNEUMA), consistent with the neuter treatment of the beast established in chapter 13.

**15:3 -- "slave of the God" vs. "servant of God":**
- EMET: "the slave of the God"
- LOGOS: "the servant of God"
- PNEUMA: "the servant of God"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "the servant of God" (LOGOS/PNEUMA). While doulos literally means "slave" (EMET), "servant of God" is the established English rendering of this honorific title applied to Moses throughout Scripture.

**15:3 -- Song opening:**
- EMET: "Great and marvelous are the works of you, Lord the God the Almighty. Righteous and true are the ways of you, the King of the nations."
- LOGOS: "Great and marvelous are your works, Lord God Almighty; righteous and true are your ways, O King of the nations."
- PNEUMA: "How great and marvelous are your works, Lord God Almighty! How just and true are your ways, O King of the nations!"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS. EMET's "the works of you" is over-literal. PNEUMA's "How great" and "How just" add exclamatory particles and "just" replaces "righteous" (dikaiai).

**15:4 -- "will not fear" vs. "shall not fear" vs. "will not stand in awe":**
- EMET: "Who will not fear, Lord, and glorify the name of you?"
- LOGOS: "Who shall not fear you, O Lord, and glorify your name?"
- PNEUMA: "Who will not stand in awe of you, Lord, and bring glory to your name?"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "Who shall not fear you, O Lord." The vocative "O Lord" (LOGOS) is appropriately liturgical. PNEUMA's "stand in awe" softens phobethē.

**15:4 -- "holy" (hosios) rendering:**
- EMET: "you alone are holy"
- LOGOS: "you alone are holy"
- PNEUMA: "you alone are holy"
- **Resolution:** All three agree on "holy" for hosios. No disagreement on the translation itself; all note the distinction from hagios in their footnotes.

**15:4 -- "righteous judgments" vs. "righteous acts":**
- EMET: "the righteous judgments of you were made manifest"
- LOGOS: "your righteous acts have been made manifest"
- PNEUMA: "your righteous judgments have been revealed for all to see"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA uses "your righteous judgments have been made manifest." The Greek dikaiomata can mean either "righteous acts" or "righteous judgments"; in context of the bowl judgments, "judgments" (EMET/PNEUMA) is preferred. "Made manifest" (EMET/LOGOS) is retained over PNEUMA's "revealed for all to see."

**15:5 -- "tent of the testimony" vs. "tabernacle of the testimony" vs. "heavenly Tent of the Testimony":**
- EMET: "the temple of the tent of the testimony in the heaven was opened"
- LOGOS: "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony in heaven was opened"
- PNEUMA: "the temple -- the heavenly Tent of the Testimony -- was thrown open"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "the temple of the tabernacle of the testimony." "Tabernacle" is the standard English rendering of skene in this Exodus-echoing context. PNEUMA's dash-parenthetical and "thrown open" are interpretive.

**15:6 -- "clean bright linen" vs. "pure bright linen" vs. "pure, brilliant linen":**
- EMET: "clean bright linen"
- LOGOS: "pure bright linen"
- PNEUMA: "pure, brilliant linen"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "pure bright linen." The Greek katharos can be "clean" (EMET) or "pure" (LOGOS/PNEUMA); "pure" is slightly more dignified for priestly garments. "Bright" (EMET/LOGOS) is preferred over PNEUMA's "brilliant."

**15:6 -- "girdles" vs. "sashes":**
- EMET: "having been girded around the chests with golden girdles"
- LOGOS: "girded around their chests with golden sashes"
- PNEUMA: "wore golden sashes across their chests"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "sashes." "Girdles" (EMET) is archaic in modern English. PNEUMA's "wore...across" simplifies the Greek participial construction.

**15:7 -- "full of the wrath" vs. "brimming with the wrath":**
- EMET: "seven golden bowls, full of the wrath of the God, the one living unto the ages of the ages"
- LOGOS: "seven golden bowls full of the wrath of God who lives forever and ever"
- PNEUMA: "seven golden bowls brimming with the wrath of God, who lives forever and ever"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows LOGOS with "full of the wrath of God who lives forever and ever." PNEUMA's "brimming" is vivid but interpretive.

**15:8 -- "until...were completed" vs. "had run their full course":**
- EMET: "until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed"
- LOGOS: "until the seven plagues of the seven angels were completed"
- PNEUMA: "until the seven plagues carried by the seven angels had run their full course"
- **Resolution:** SOPHIA follows EMET/LOGOS with "were completed." PNEUMA's "had run their full course" is a dynamic paraphrase of telestosin.

---

-- SOPHIA
